sections.title: What conditionals do (core logic)
sections.content:
Conditionals link two clauses — **if X, then Y** — and the tense pair around *if* tells the listener how **real** or **unreal** it is.
- The most powerful clause-linker English has — heart of the **Clause Linking** root (#4 in the Tree Model), pulling in the **Verb Phrase** root: each type uses a specific tense combination.
- Master the four tense-pairings and you can speculate, hypothesise, regret, warn, promise and negotiate — the high-stakes moves in conversation and writing.

diagrams.title: Two simple rules that solve most errors

items.good: If it rains…
items.why: no *will* in an if-clause for the first conditional

items.good: If I had time…
items.why: no *would* in an if-clause for the second / third

diagrams.text: *kdyby* covers both second and third conditional in Czech. In English you must pick: **past tense** for present/future hypotheticals (*if I had…*) vs **past perfect** for past hypotheticals (*if I had had…*). Choosing the wrong one is the #1 conditional error.

sections.title: The four types at a glance
sections.content:
Learn the **four tense-pairings** and the **use** that goes with each — everything else is detail.
The tense pair alone signals the speaker's stance toward reality: wrong tense pair = wrong **meaning**, not just wrong grammar.

diagrams.title: The four conditional types
rows: If it **rains**, I'**ll stay** at home.
If I **had** time, I **would help** you.
If I **had known**, I **would have told** you.

sections.title: Which conditional do I need?
sections.content:
Ask the questions in order — the first *yes* gives your type.
*If I see him, I'll tell him* = First (one-off future event); *if you press this button, the light comes on* = Zero (general law of behaviour).

steps.q: A general law of nature / behaviour — always true?
Imaginary / contrary to fact right now?
steps.yes: Second: if + past simple, would + base
Third: if + past perfect, would have + past participle

steps.end: Condition and result in different time-frames → Mixed conditional

sections.title: Real conditionals — Zero & First
sections.content:
The **real** conditionals treat the situation as actually possible or factually true — everyday cause-and-effect, plans, warnings, instructions.
They are the foundation — get these solid before moving to the unreal types.

left.title: Zero — general truth
points: If you **mix** red and blue, you **get** purple.

right.title: First — real future
points: if + present; **will + base** (or imperative) in the main clause
If it **rains** tomorrow, I'**ll stay** home.

diagrams.text: Don't put *will* in the if-clause. Czech uses the future (*bude pršet*) inside *jestli/když*; English does not. Say *If it **rains**, I will stay* — never *if it will rain*.

sections.title: Unreal present — Second conditional
sections.content:
**If + past simple, would + base.** The past doesn't mean past time — it signals **distance from reality**: imaginary or contrary to fact now.
Workhorse phrases: *If I were you…*, *If I had a million crowns…*, *If we lived in Italy…* Exam tasks (sentence transformation, CAE/FCE Use of English) lean on it heavily.

items.good: If I had more time, I would help you.
items.why: I don't have time (now); helping is hypothetical

items.good: If we lived in Prague, we would see each other more.
items.why: we don't live there

sections.title: Second conditional — *were* & Czech traps
sections.content: Formal English uses **were** for all persons in the if-clause — and never *would* after *if*.

items.good: If I were you, I would apologise.
items.bad: If I was you, I would apologise.
items.why: formal *were* for all persons — you'll hear *was* in informal speech, but exams and writing expect *were*

items.good: If he were here, he would explain.
items.why: *were* even with he/she/it

items.good: If I had time, I would help.
items.bad: If I would have time, I would help.
items.why: don't translate *kdybych měl* literally — past simple in the if-clause, never *would*

diagrams.text: The English past tense after *if* is **not** a real past — it's the unreal-marker. *If I knew* = *Kdybych věděl*, not *Když jsem věděl*.

sections.title: Unreal past — Third conditional
sections.content:
**If + past perfect, would have + past participle** — past that **did not happen**: regrets, counterfactuals, alternative histories.
The grammar of hindsight, blame and what-might-have-been — high frequency in storytelling, interviews and exam writing tasks.

items.label: Pattern
items.formula: If + past perfect → would have + past participle

items.good: If I had known, I would have told you.
items.why: but I didn't know, so I didn't tell you

items.good: If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train.
items.why: she didn't leave early; she missed it

items.good: If you hadn't helped me, I would have failed.
items.why: you did help; I didn't fail

diagrams.title: You're reaching for it every time you say

sections.title: Third conditional — Czech traps
sections.content: *Would have* belongs only in the main clause — and a pure past condition needs the full past structure in **both** clauses.

items.good: If I had known, I would have come.
items.bad: If I would have known, I would have come.
items.why: no *would have* in the if-clause

items.good: If I had known, I would have told you.
items.bad: If I had known, I would tell you.
items.why: don't mix Third and Second: past condition + present result only works as a Mixed conditional — pure past needs the past-perfect / would-have structure in both clauses

sections.title: Mixed conditionals
sections.content:
**Mixed conditionals** combine a condition and a result from **different time-frames** — usually past condition, present result, or vice versa.
Real life isn't always purely past or purely present: the most common mix — *past cause → present consequence* — is essential for talking about how earlier choices shape your current situation. Common in CAE/FCE and any reflective conversation about life decisions.

items.label: Present → past
items.formula: If + past simple → would have + past participle

items.good: If I had studied harder at school, I would have a better job now.
items.why: past condition → present result

items.good: If she hadn't moved to Prague, we wouldn't be together today.
items.why: past condition → present result

items.good: If I were more careful, I wouldn't have lost my keys yesterday.
items.why: present condition → past result

items.good: If he weren't so shy, he would have asked her out.
items.why: present condition → past result

diagrams.title: Time markers that often signal a mix

sections.title: Wishes & if only
sections.content:
**Wishes** use the same tense-distancing logic — past for present, past perfect for past — to express **regret**, **longing** or **complaint**.
- The emotional layer of conditionals: what you want to be true but isn't, or what you'd undo if you could. High frequency in conversation and writing.
- Present wish = Second-conditional logic; past wish = Third.
- *would* + base = about someone else's behaviour you can't control.

diagrams.title: Three patterns
rows: I wish I **had** more time. / If only I **knew** the answer.
I wish I **had studied** harder. / If only she **hadn't said** that.
I wish you **would stop** interrupting. / If only it **would rain**.

diagrams.text: *Kéž by* covers all three; English splits them by tense. And: you can't say *I wish I would…* about yourself — only about other people or impersonal forces.

sections.title: Variants — unless, as long as, provided that…
sections.content:
*If* isn't the only word that introduces a conditional — these alternatives add shades of **restriction**, **exception**, **precondition**.
They appear constantly in formal writing, contracts, instructions and CAE-level reading — more precise conditions than *if* alone.

diagrams.title: The main set
rows: I'll be there at 7 **unless** the train is late. (= if it isn't late)
You can borrow it **as long as** you return it tomorrow.
We'll sign **provided that** the terms don't change.
as a precaution against — **not** the same as *if*
Take an umbrella **in case** it rains. (= take it now, just in case; *take it if it rains* = only take it then)
**Supposing** he doesn't come, what will we do?

items.good: unless you tell him
items.why: Czech-speaker trap: *unless* is one negative idea, not two — it already means *if you don't tell him*

sections.title: Quick reference — if-clause traps
sections.content:
Most Czech-speaker conditional errors come from word-for-word translation into the if-clause.
Once you see the pattern, you can self-correct in real time.

items.good: If I see him, I'll tell him.
items.bad: If I will see him, I'll tell him.
items.why: First — no *will* in the if-clause

items.good: If I had time, I would help.
items.bad: If I would have time, I would help.
items.why: Second — no *would* in the if-clause

items.good: If I had known, I would have come.
items.bad: If I would have known, I would have come.
items.why: Third — no *would have* in the if-clause

sections.title: Quick reference — more Czech traps
sections.content: The rest of the top traps — these account for the large majority of conditional mistakes, and fixing them gives immediate visible progress.

items.good: If I had known, I would have told you.
items.bad: If I had known, I would tell you.
items.why: Third — both clauses need the past-perfect / would-have structure

items.good: If I were you…
items.why: Second — formal *were*, not *was*, in writing and exams

items.good: unless you tell him
items.why: *unless* — no double negative

diagrams.text: *až* ≠ *until*: Czech *až přijde* often means *when he comes*, not *until he comes*. Check the meaning before translating.

questions.question: Which type of conditional is: 'If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.'?
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present + present for general/scientific facts.

questions.question: Choose the correct first conditional.

options.a: If it will rain, we will stay at home.
options.b: If it rained, we would stay at home.
options.c: If it rains, we will stay at home.
options.d: If it rains, we would stay at home.

questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present, will + base. No 'will' in the if-clause.

questions.question: 'If I _____ you, I would apologise.' (Second conditional)
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past. 'Were' is used for all persons in formal style (subjunctive).

questions.question: Which is a third conditional?

options.a: If I know, I will tell you.
options.b: If I had known, I would tell you.
options.c: If I knew, I would have told you.
options.d: If I had known, I would have told you.

questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle (unreal past).

questions.question: What is wrong? 'If I would have time, I would help you.'

options.a: We must use 'had' in the if-clause, not 'would have'.
options.b: We must use 'will' in the main clause.
options.c: We must use past perfect in both clauses.

questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (if I had time), not would have.

questions.question: 'If she _____ earlier, she would have caught the train.' (Third conditional)
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect (had left).

questions.question: Zero: If you heat ice, it _____.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: if + present, present (it melts).

questions.question: First: If it _____ tomorrow, we will stay at home.
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (rains), will + base.

questions.question: Second: If I _____ more time, I would help you.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (had), would + base.

questions.question: Second: If I were you, I _____ apologise.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: would + base verb (would apologise).

questions.question: Third: If I _____ known, I would have told you.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect (had known).

questions.question: Third: If she had left earlier, she _____ the train.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle.

questions.question: First: If you _____ early, call me.
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (finish), main clause can be imperative.

questions.question: Zero: If you don't sleep well, you _____ tired.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present in both clauses (you feel).

questions.question: Second: If we _____ the lottery, we would travel the world.
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (won).

questions.question: Third: If they hadn't missed the flight, they _____ on time.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle (would have arrived).

questions.question: Zero: If you heat ice, it _____.
answers: melts
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present + present for facts.

questions.question: First: If it _____ tomorrow, we will stay at home.
answers: rains
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present (rains), will in main clause.

questions.question: Second: If I _____ (have) more time, I would help you.
answers: had
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple.

questions.question: Second: If I were you, I _____ (apologise).
answers: would apologise
questions.explanation: Second conditional: would + base in main clause.

questions.question: Third: If I _____ (know), I would have told you.
answers: had known
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect.

questions.question: Third: If she had left earlier, she _____ (catch) the train.
answers: would have caught
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle in main clause.

questions.question: First: If you _____ early, call me. (one word; present simple, e.g. finish or leave)
answers: finish
arrive
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple; main clause can be imperative. Common verbs: finish, arrive.

questions.question: Zero: If you don't sleep well, you _____ tired.
answers: feel
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: present in both clauses.

questions.question: Second: If we _____ (win) the lottery, we would travel the world.
answers: won
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (won, not would win).

questions.question: Third: If they hadn't missed the flight, they _____ (arrive) on time.
answers: would have arrived
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have + past participle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I will see him, I'll tell him.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I see him, I'll tell him.
If I see him, I'll tell him
questions.explanation: First conditional: no 'will' in the if-clause; use present.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I would have time, I would help.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had time, I would help.
If I had time, I would help
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (had), not would have.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I would have known, I would have come.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had known, I would have come.
If I had known, I would have come
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if-clause uses past perfect (had known), not would have.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If I had known, I would tell you.' (Meaning: I didn't know, so I didn't tell.) Correct the sentence.
answers: If I had known, I would have told you.
If I had known, I would have told you
questions.explanation: Third conditional: both clauses refer to the past – would have told.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If it will rain, we will stay at home.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If it rains, we will stay at home.
If it rains, we will stay at home
questions.explanation: First conditional: present in if-clause (if it rains).

questions.question: Wrong: 'If she had left earlier, she would catch the train.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train.
If she had left earlier, she would have caught the train
questions.explanation: Third conditional: main clause needs would have + past participle.

questions.question: Wrong: 'If you heat water to 100 degrees, it will boil.' (General fact.) Correct to zero conditional.
answers: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils
questions.explanation: Zero conditional for general truth: present + present (it boils).

questions.question: Wrong: 'If he would try harder, he could pass.' Correct the sentence.
answers: If he tried harder, he could pass.
If he tried harder, he could pass
questions.explanation: Second conditional: if-clause uses past simple (tried), not would try.

mc.title: Practice: Conditionals (zero, first, second, third) (multiple choice)
